title: China Is Sabotaging the World That Enables Its Rise
gist: China's rise has always depended on exploiting the Soviet-led and then U.S.-led orders rather than building an alternative to them, and Beijing's triumphalist 'East is rising, West is declining' narrative blinds it to the risk that its export-driven overcapacity model is now provoking the very Western and non-Western protectionist backlash that could undercut its own ascent.

themes.theme: The core paradox: rising by exploiting the order it wants to replace

insights.claim: China's rise has not happened outside the international systems its rivals built, but through Beijing's skillful exploitation of them: Communist China relied on Soviet assistance in its early decades, and since Deng Xiaoping's 'reform and opening' policies in the late 1970s it has used access to Western markets to forge itself into an economic and military superpower.
insights.speaker: Enrico Fardella
insights.so_what: Reframes China's rise as externally enabled rather than self-generated — a lens for judging how vulnerable that rise is if external access is withdrawn.
insights.locator: China's rise has not happened outside the systems its rivals built

insights.claim: Unlike the Soviet Union, China has never built a comprehensive alternative order capable of replacing U.S.-led institutions; instead it has relied on the openness of the existing order while progressively eroding its rules from within, meaning the more China weakens the liberal order, the more it undermines the foundation its own prosperity depends on.
insights.speaker: Enrico Fardella
insights.so_what: This is the article's central thesis: China's own strategy is self-undermining over the long run, a key variable for multi-year positioning on China's global standing.
insights.locator: Unlike the Soviet Union, China has never constructed

insights.claim: At the World Economic Forum session in Dalian in June 2026, instead of acknowledging China relies on the willingness of mainly Western countries to keep absorbing its surplus, Premier Li Qiang asserted that China is simply more competitive because it invests in innovation.
insights.speaker: Enrico Fardella
insights.so_what: A concrete, dated example of Beijing's public refusal to acknowledge its overcapacity problem — evidence the blind spot is official policy, not just analyst inference.
insights.quote: China is simply more competitive because it invests in innovation
insights.locator: at the World Economic Forum session in Dalian in June

insights.claim: Xi Jinping has publicly adopted the mantra 'the East is Rising, the West is declining' (东升西降), a slogan whose intellectual roots trace to the 2007-08 global financial crisis, which many Chinese analysts interpreted as proof of structural Western weakness.
insights.speaker: Enrico Fardella
insights.so_what: Identifies the specific ideological framework driving Chinese strategic patience and risk tolerance toward the West.
insights.quote: the East is Rising, the West is declining
insights.locator: In recent years, Xi has publicly adopted the mantra

insights.claim: Viral Chinese memes since late 2025 about an 'American kill line' — the idea that ordinary Americans are living one crisis from collapse — have spread the perception among younger Chinese that China's problems are temporary while the West's are structural.
insights.speaker: Enrico Fardella
insights.so_what: A grassroots narrative reinforcing elite triumphalism, making internal Chinese course-correction on economic imbalances politically harder to sell.
insights.locator: Since late 2025, viral Chinese memes

insights.claim: In Xi's strategic calculus, the 'Thucydides Trap' can be avoided because the United States is expected to weaken internally before any catastrophic conflict becomes unavoidable, so China's strategy is to outlast and out-adapt the U.S. rather than confront it directly, and to persuade a growing number of states that Chinese leadership offers a more legitimate basis for international order.
insights.speaker: Enrico Fardella
insights.so_what: China's core bet is patience over confrontation, premised on an assumption of US internal decline — a falsifiable assumption worth tracking against actual US economic and political trajectory.
insights.locator: In Beijing's view, China can supplant the U.S.-led order

insights.claim: Beijing believes China can succeed where the Soviet Union failed because 'Socialism with Chinese Characteristics' provides the civilizational depth, political resilience, and economic dynamism needed to sustain the long economic and ideological struggle required to supersede American hegemony.
insights.speaker: Enrico Fardella
insights.so_what: Explains the ideological self-confidence underpinning Beijing's willingness to absorb short-term trade friction in pursuit of long-term primacy.
insights.locator: Buttressing these assumptions is Beijing's belief

themes.theme: The Belt and Road and the narrative's global reach

insights.claim: The Belt and Road Initiative, launched in 2013 and now extending to more than 150 countries, is Beijing's vehicle for shifting the international economy's center of gravity toward a China-centered network of standards, supply chains, and financial infrastructure, away from the post-WWII institutions underpinning U.S. leadership.
insights.speaker: Enrico Fardella
insights.so_what: Frames BRI explicitly as an inside-out institutional-displacement strategy, not just an infrastructure-financing program.
insights.locator: Take the Belt and Road Initiative

insights.claim: China's 'East is rising, West is declining' narrative has gained traction outside the West in countries as dissimilar as Cambodia, Zimbabwe, Iran, and Saudi Arabia, and within the West among figures like Hungary's Viktor Orban and Slovakia's Robert Fico, as well as business and political circles in France, Italy, and parts of Germany's industrial establishment.
insights.speaker: Enrico Fardella
insights.so_what: China's narrative has real political penetration inside the EU itself (Hungary, Slovakia, and pockets of France/Italy/Germany), a structural obstacle to unified European trade-defense action.
insights.locator: The Chinese argument has also gained a foothold

insights.claim: Recurrent Trump-EU tensions over trade, defense spending, NATO, and tariffs have reinforced Beijing's perception that the transatlantic relationship is fragile and that Europe may eventually seek greater distance from Washington, an assumption central to China's patience-based strategy.
insights.speaker: Enrico Fardella
insights.so_what: US-EU friction directly feeds Beijing's strategic confidence, making transatlantic unity a variable that shapes China's own risk-taking.
insights.locator: Recurrent tensions between Trump and European leaders

themes.theme: Economic imbalance and dependence on foreign absorption

insights.claim: Leading Chinese economists and former central bankers increasingly agree China's core economic problem is insufficient domestic demand — excess savings, weak household consumption, and chronic demand deficiency from an investment-led growth model — yet they remain largely silent about China's dependence on foreign markets absorbing its surplus.
insights.speaker: Enrico Fardella
insights.so_what: Domestic Chinese economic diagnosis is converging on the demand-deficiency problem even as the external-dependence half of the story remains politically unaddressed.
insights.locator: In recent months, leading Chinese economists

insights.claim: Economist Michael Pettis argues China's suppressed consumption and excess savings force it to export both goods and capital, historically requiring the United States above all to absorb Chinese surpluses through trade deficits and capital inflows.
insights.speaker: Enrico Fardella
insights.so_what: The Pettis framework is the standard lens for tracing how Chinese domestic imbalances mechanically transmit into other countries' trade and capital accounts.
insights.locator: Michael Pettis, a Beijing-based economist, has long argued

insights.claim: European analysts argue that as U.S. efforts to reduce Chinese import dependence accelerate, Beijing is diverting excess production toward European markets, exposing the EU to rising deindustrialization and deflationary competition that will fuel unemployment, inequality, and support for nationalist and far-right political movements across Europe.
insights.speaker: Enrico Fardella
insights.so_what: Links Chinese trade diversion directly to a specific political outcome — the rise of European far-right movements — a channel worth tracking in European election forecasting.
insights.locator: European analysts are now extending this argument to the EU

insights.claim: Europe is structurally more vulnerable than the U.S. to absorbing China's imbalances because the eurozone combines export-surplus economies like Germany and the Netherlands with import-dependent Southern European economies, creating internal divergence that China can exploit to fracture a unified EU response.
insights.speaker: Enrico Fardella
insights.so_what: Explains why EU-China trade policy is harder to coordinate than US-China policy — a durable structural handicap, not just a political-will problem.
insights.locator: As Michael Pettis and Enrico Fardella have argued

insights.claim: As the U.S. moves to limit Chinese market access, Europe is becoming the last major dumping ground for China's surpluses, with persistent trade imbalances set to accelerate debt accumulation, deindustrialization, and political fragmentation across the continent.
insights.speaker: Enrico Fardella
insights.so_what: As US-China decoupling proceeds, expect the trade-imbalance pressure to concentrate increasingly on Europe rather than dissipate globally.
insights.quote: Europe is becoming the last major dumping ground for China's surpluses
insights.locator: As the United States moves to limit China's access

insights.claim: A bipartisan U.S. consensus has emerged around reducing dependence on Chinese supply chains and countering subsidized Chinese overcapacity, while the European Commission has officially declared the current state of EU-China economic relations 'unsustainable.'
insights.speaker: Enrico Fardella
insights.so_what: Both major Western blocs have moved past debate into consensus that current China-trade terms must change — a bipartisan/trans-Atlantic policy tailwind for de-risking measures.
insights.locator: Already, Beijing's approach has accelerated protectionist responses

insights.claim: Backlash against Chinese industrial dominance is spreading beyond the West: India is expanding trade-defense instruments and investment screening on Chinese firms, Brazil and Turkey are intensifying anti-dumping investigations, and Indonesia is working to reduce dependence on Chinese manufactured imports.
insights.speaker: Enrico Fardella
insights.so_what: China's overcapacity problem is generating a genuinely global protectionist response, not just a US-EU one, narrowing Beijing's remaining export outlets.
insights.locator: Even countries outside the West are beginning to follow suit

insights.claim: China's growth model is trapped in a self-reinforcing contradiction: the same low-cost, subsidized export machine that fueled its ascent is now generating the international protectionism, de-risking, and fragmentation that will constrain its future growth.
insights.speaker: Enrico Fardella
insights.so_what: The article's core forward-looking call: China's export model is approaching diminishing, then negative, returns as global tolerance for absorbing its surplus runs out.
insights.locator: The more Beijing seeks to increase industrial dominance

themes.theme: The path out, or the trap

insights.claim: If Europe and the U.S. successfully close off external markets for China's excess production, Beijing would be forced to rebalance toward household consumption — 'socializing prosperity' rather than production — requiring a politically costly transfer of resources from businesses and local governments to households.
insights.speaker: Enrico Fardella
insights.so_what: Defines the specific, high-cost domestic reform (income redistribution to households) that would be required for China to escape the trap — a benchmark for judging whether future Chinese stimulus is cosmetic or structural.
insights.locator: In principle, this could produce the most constructive outcome

insights.claim: The authors warn that if Beijing refuses to heed Western warnings and instead treats its rise as historical destiny rather than the product of a favorable external environment, it risks triggering a vicious trade war that will leave the world much worse off and derail China's own national rejuvenation.
insights.speaker: Enrico Fardella
insights.so_what: The closing thesis and highest-stakes scenario in the piece: a full-blown trade war is the downside case if Beijing stays on its current course.
insights.quote: it will trigger a vicious trade war that will leave the world much worse off
insights.locator: Yet history offers a cautionary lesson
